locked_facts: Electric potential is a SCALAR — it has no direction
Electric field is negative gradient of potential: E = -dV/dr
Zero potential ≠ zero field — they are independent quantities
Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to field lines
Potential due to point charge: V = kq/r
truth_statements: The electric potential at a point is the work done per unit positive charge in bringing it from infinity to that point
Equipotential surfaces for a point charge are concentric spheres
No work is done in moving a charge along an equipotential surface
The electric field always points from higher potential to lower potential
For a dipole, the potential is zero at every point on the perpendicular bisector (equatorial plane)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show the equatorial plane of the dipole where V = 0 everywhere, but E is clearly non-zero (field lines pass through it at full strength). This directly contradicts the 'zero V means zero E' misconception.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Highlight the equatorial plane as a surface, color it differently. Show field lines passing right through it — strong field, zero potential.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see a region where potential is exactly zero but field lines are dense and strong — V and E are truly independent

simulation_states.label: Dipole field lines — from + to −
simulation_states.description: Electric dipole: +q and -q separated. Field lines curve from positive to negative charge. 3D view shows the full spatial pattern.
simulation_states.emphasis: Field lines emerge from +q and terminate at -q — they are NOT random curves

simulation_states.label: Equipotential surfaces appear
simulation_states.description: Wireframe equipotential surfaces surround each charge — not perfect spheres because the other charge distorts them. The equatorial plane (V=0) is highlighted.
simulation_states.emphasis: Notice: the equipotential surface shapes are distorted by the second charge. They are no longer perfect spheres.

simulation_states.label: Zero potential ≠ zero field
simulation_states.description: Camera rotates to show the equatorial plane (V=0) edge-on. Field lines clearly pass through it at full strength. Caption: V=0 here, but E is NOT zero!
simulation_states.emphasis: THIS is the key insight: V=0 everywhere on this plane, but E is clearly non-zero — look at the dense field lines passing through!

simulation_states.label: Explore — rotate the dipole
simulation_states.description: Full interactive mode. All field lines, equipotential surfaces, and the V=0 plane visible. Student rotates freely.
simulation_states.emphasis: Rotate to see: field lines always cross equipotential surfaces at right angles. V is scalar, E is vector — they are different quantities.

trigger_keywords: potential zero hai toh field nahi hoga
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Zero electric potential means zero electric field there
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Potential and field are related but distinct quantities. E = -dV/dr means the field depends on the RATE OF CHANGE of potential, not its absolute value. The equatorial plane of a dipole has V=0 everywhere but E is clearly non-zero.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the V=0 equatorial plane of a dipole with field lines passing through it at full strength.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: Electric potential has a direction like electric field
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Electric potential is a SCALAR quantity — it has magnitude but no direction. It is the work done per unit charge. Only the electric field E is a vector.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show equipotential surfaces as uniform-value shells. There is no arrow on them — potential is just a number at each point.

typical_question_types: find V at a point due to dipole
relationship between E and V for given geometry
work done moving charge on equipotential
